chunks.text: Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-60162-0 Compositional criteria to predict columnar to equiaxed transitions in metal additive manufacturing
Received: 13 October 2024
Ryan Brooke 1, Duyao Zhang 1,2 , Dong Qiu 1, Mark A. Gibson1 & Mark Easton 1
Accepted: 16 May 2025
Check for updates

Predicting the columnar to equiaxed transition (CET) and grain reﬁnement for additively manufactured alloys from thermodynamic databases has been a long-standing challenge and an ongoing source of discussion. Efforts are focused on designing alloy compositions to achieve fully equiaxed microstructures, thereby eliminating the mechanical anisotropy commonly associated with the large columnar grains in additively manufactured alloys. Here, three compositional parameters proposed in the literature are evaluated across a range of Ti alloys: the non-equilibrium solidiﬁcation range (ΔTs), the growth restriction factor (Q) and constitutional supercooling parameter (P). Ti-Fe, Ti-Cu, Ti-Cu-Fe, and Ti-Mo alloys produced via direct energy deposition experimentally veriﬁed that P is the most reliable parameter to guide the selection of alloying elements for additively manufactured (AM) alloys. Veriﬁcation was found by reconsidering results from additional alloy systems and AM methods. The numerical CET models also predict that P is closely related to dendrite tip undercooling at high growth velocities, as found in AM. This work provides a clearer framework for predicting the grain morphology of metallic alloys in AM.

examined in the context of metal AM processes as follows: the constitutional supercooling (CS) parameter (P), the growth restriction factor (Q), and the non-equilibrium (Scheil) freezing range (ΔTs). P is the constitutional supercooling available during steady-state solidiﬁcation8,9, therefore, theoretically it would be expected to be important in applications where nucleation happens after solidiﬁcation is well-established, such as in directional solidiﬁcation. It was the ﬁrst parameter considered to have a relationship with grain size in cast alloys9,10. In a binary system, the supercooling parameter P is calculated as:
P = mcoðk  1Þ k ð1Þ

It is well established that the ability of an alloy to undergo a CET and achieve a reﬁned, equiaxed grain morphology is inﬂuenced by the alloying composition during solidiﬁcation processes such as casting1–3. However, at the high cooling rate and large thermal gradients observed in fusion-based metal AM, there is a greater propensity to form textured columnar grains4. An equiaxed microstructure is generally preferred to eliminate the mechanical anisotropy associated with coarse columnar grains5. Simple compositional parameters have been proposed to indicate the propensity of an alloy to achieve a ﬁne-equiaxed microstructure over a range of solidiﬁcation processes. While each compositional parameter is supported by some experimental results3,6, debate persists about their respective efﬁcacies7. Developing a reliable CET indicator is a critical step to guide alloy design for high-performance metal AM components. In this paper, the most common CET indicators are

Nature Communications| (2025) 16:5710 1
software with the PanTitaniumTM v2022 database. The representation of the ΔTs (grey range), Q (blue range), and P (green range) values for Ti-6Cu from the phase diagram.

Ti alloy has an equiaxed grain morphology. ΔTs is the freezing range calculated from CALPHAD software. It was suggested that a Ti alloy with a ΔTs considerably higher than 110 K and perhaps closer to 603 K would achieve a CET7. Although Nartu et al.7 did not explicitly provide the detailed methodology for calculating ΔTs, the values reported correlate to the non-equilibrium (Scheil) solidiﬁcation freezing range as calculated by PANDATTM (PanTi2022 database) (see Fig. 1). The research presented here has been provoked by recent ﬁndings25 in which decreasing ΔTs is correlated with the CET and grain reﬁnement in Ti-xCu-yFe alloys, contradicting the ﬁndings of Nartu et al.7. Hence the current study aims to provide a comparison between these three composition related parameters, ΔTs, Q and P, across a range of titanium alloys produced via laser beam directed energy deposition (DED-LB) and their efﬁcacy to predict the grain morphology and grain reﬁnement in AM. Titanium alloys were chosen as a case study as their CET is a common research focus. As the implemented process parameters inﬂuence grain reﬁnement26, these were initially kept constant to isolate compositional effects. It is also worth noting that the powder size distribution can additionally inﬂuence the solidiﬁcation conditions27, therefore, the feedstock powder distribution was kept constant. Three Ti alloy systems with increasing solute content were produced to assess the changing values of ΔTs, Q, and P and their associated correlation to the CET and grain reﬁnement (see Table S1). Two binary Ti-Cu alloys, two binary Ti-Fe alloys, and two ternary Ti-Cu-Fe alloys were investigated at two different levels of Q (i.e., ~42 K and ~93 K). Additionally, Ti-4Fe and Ti−12Mo alloys were produced at different process parameters to understand the inﬂuence of solidiﬁcation conditions on the grain morphology and grain size. Ti-Mo was chosen owing to its distinctive correlation between Q and P values, i.e., Q > P, in contrast to the other common alloys where Q < P. This is due to k being greater than 1 in the Ti-Mo system. Furthermore, a substantial data set of Ti, Cu, and Al alloys from the literature, where the values of ΔTs, Q, and P are compared with the corresponding parent β-grain morphology, is also analysed.

where m is the liquidus slope, co the alloy content, and k is the partition coefﬁcient, i.e., the ratio cs/cl where cs is the composition of the solute in the solid and cl is the composition in the liquid. The growth restriction factor, Q, was identiﬁed initially as a key parameter to describe the reduction in dendritic growth rate, as a result of solutal undercooling11. Later work deﬁned Q as the initial rate of development of constitutional supercooling at the beginning of solidiﬁcation8,12 indicating it would be more effective for systems where there is copious nucleation early in solidiﬁcation, such as in systems with potent grain reﬁners. There are many examples that correlate the grain size with the 1/Q value in cast Mg2,13, Al1,14, and Ti alloys15,16. The growth restriction factor Q is calculated by:
Q = Pk = mcoðk  1Þ ð2Þ

The Hunt CET model17 suggested that a higher Q facilitates equiaxed growth. Later, Kurz et al.18 produced a simpliﬁcation of a numerical CET model that considered the equilibrium freezing range, P, instead. The Hunt analytical model17 was developed to describe the CET of conventional castings. The numerical model by Kurz et al. & Gäumann et al.18–21 was developed to describe the CET during rapid solidiﬁcation, which could be more applicable to AM. Q and P can be taken directly from the phase diagram for binary alloys, as shown in Fig. 1 for a binary Ti-6Cu alloy (all compositions in this paper are given in weight percent (wt.%)). For the alloys containing multiple alloying elements22, Q, can be estimated from the sum of the contributions from each alloying element, or more accurately from CALculation of PHAse Diagrams (CALPHAD) data using:
Q = dΔTc df s

where ΔTc is the constitutional supercooling, and fs is the solid fraction. Q has been successfully used as an alloy design parameter for AM alloys23,24. P can also be estimated in multicomponent systems using CALPHAD. Recently, Nartu et al.7 proposed another parameter, denoted here as ΔTs, which was claimed to be more effective at predicting whether a

Results The grain sizes for the Ti-Fe, Ti-Cu-Fe, Ti-Cu, and Ti-Mo alloys are listed in Table S1. It can be seen in Fig. 2a–d that the Ti-Fe and Ti-Cu-Fe alloys, respectively, have similar grain morphologies. The lower Q alloys (Ti4Fe and Ti-2.4Cu-2.4Fe) have similar average grain size, and each has a mixed columnar and equiaxed microstructure. At higher Q, the Ti-8Fe and Ti-4.7Cu-4.7Fe alloys have fully equiaxed microstructures with similar grain size. Both increases in Q also correlate to an increase in P, as both values are related to the CS characteristics of the alloy. As the solute content in the Ti-Fe and Ti-Cu-Fe alloys increases and grain reﬁnement occurs, the corresponding ΔTs decrease. Increasing Q and P also correlate with a dramatic reduction in crystallographic texture, as shown in the pole ﬁgures of Fig. 2a–d. At the higher Q (95 K) for Ti-12.6Cu, a hyper-eutectoid composition, parent β-grain analysis indicates a very reﬁned grain size of 6.2μm (Fig. S1). This is an order of magnitude smaller than the Ti-8Fe and Ti4.7Cu-4.7Fe alloys. However, recent research has shown the difﬁculty associated with parent grain reconstruction of hyper-eutectoid Ti-Cu alloys28, and it is considered that some additional β-grain nucleation is likely to occur during the cyclic heating associated with AM29 due to the active eutectoid transformation. This phenomenon is further supported by the observations of the ﬁnal layer of deposition of Ti12.6Cu. Clear parent β-grain boundaries can be seen by hypereutectoid Ti2Cu formation at these boundaries, with larger grain sizes of 50–150 µm as shown in Fig. S2a, b. Therefore, the reconstructed β-grain size may be unreliable as an indication of the original grain size in Ti-Cu alloys. Figure 2f shows a higher magniﬁcation ﬁeld of the ﬁnal layer of deposition, with traced grain boundaries. Being the ﬁnal layer of deposition, thermal cycling has not occurred, therefore

Aztec Crystal software. f The backscattered electron (BSE) image depicts the traced parent β-grains marked by hyper-eutectoid Ti2Cu, note the increased magniﬁcation of Ti-12.6Cu (f).

the additional β-grain nucleation has also been avoided. This top layer of deposition of Ti-12.6Cu has smaller grains (65 µm) compared with the parent β-grain reconstruction of Ti-8Fe and Ti-4.7Cu-4.7Fe from the centre of the sample. At the lower Q (43 K), Ti-6Cu has more columnar grains as indicated by the aspect ratio (area weighted) of 4.00 compared to the mixed columnar and equiaxed microstructures of Ti-4Fe and Ti-2.4Cu-2.4Fe.

columnar to a mixture of columnar and equiaxed to a fully equiaxed microstructure (see Fig. 5a for ΔTs, Q, and P). Welk et al.33 also showed that the addition of 3% Fe content to a Ti-5.5Al-5V-5Mo-2.4Cr-0.75Fe0.15 O (Ti-18) master alloy induces some CET, which correlates with an increasing ΔTs, Q, and P. Table S3 provides the measures of ΔTs, Q, and P, alongside the grain morphology for these alloys. As Nartu et al.7 pointed out, when using ΔTs, veriﬁcation and accuracy of the databases will be important. Figure 5a, b shows the variation between PANDATTM (PanTi2022 database) and the JMatPro (Titanium alloy database) for calculating ΔTs. Using the JMatPro database, ΔTs increases until the eutectic reaction occurs; in these two cases, at Ti64-1.5Ni and Ti-2.1Cu. There is potential utility in identifying the maximum amount of solute for reﬁnement without inducing a secondary phase. This method was successfully employed by Sui et al.34, where reﬁnement of Ti-6Al-4V was achieved with the addition of 0.9Ni without introducing the Ti2Ni intermetallic phase. The correlation of ΔTs with grain size in the alloys produced by Nartu et al.7 is mostly due to the Ti-Cu alloys. Research by Zhang et al.23

the Ti-Fe and Ti-Cu-Fe alloys showing similar values. The increasing Q and P values also correlate to a CET and grain reﬁnement for the Ti-Cu alloys, however the difﬁculty in identifying the parent β-grains from the bulk region makes the comparison to the other alloys difﬁcult. Research in DED-LB26 and electron beam powder bed fusion (PBFEB)32 illustrates the inﬂuence that solidiﬁcation conditions have on CET and grain reﬁnement. It is therefore important to consider how process parameters affect the CET. Further Ti-4Fe samples were produced at a laser power of 600 W and 1200 W, with the other process parameters unchanged. As shown in Fig. 4a, b, changing the solidiﬁcation conditions induces a CET. At 600 W laser power, a fully equiaxed microstructure is achieved, whereas at 1200 W power, a coarse columnar microstructure is observed. In the 600 W Ti-4Fe sample (Fig. 4a), some incidence of partially melted Ti powder is present, however these do not appear to be the source of reﬁnement, as the equiaxed grains are throughout the bulk and not wholly concentrated near these particles. This has similarly been shown in other Ti alloys26. Ti-12Mo has a higher Q but a much lower P than Ti-4Fe. Ti-12Mo displays columnar grains at both 600 W and 1200 W as shown in Fig. 4c, d.

Discussion With process parameters having an inﬂuence on the ﬁnal microstructure for DED-LB Ti alloys, it becomes difﬁcult to compare datasets when produced under different conditions. It has been shown in this research that increasing the solute level for Ti-Cu, Ti-Fe, and Ti-Cu-Fe leads to a CET while maintaining set process parameters. Previous work25 similarly showed that Ti-Cu-Fe alloys undergo a CET with increasing solute content, correlating with decreasing ΔTs, and increasing Q and P (see Table S2). Work by Welk et al.33 showed that increasing the Ni addition to a Ti-6Al-4V master alloy will also lead to a CET. The addition of 0.5% to 1.7% and then 3.5% Ni transforms the microstructure from coarse

also produced fully equiaxed microstructures of Ti-(3.5, 6.5, and 8.5)Cu alloys. Table S4 provides the measures for ΔTs, Q, and P for both data sets. Zhang found that the increasing Cu content reduced the average grain size, which correlates to a decreasing ΔTs and increasing Q and P. However, it has been shown in Figs. S1 and S2 that the reconstructed grain size likely includes additional grain reﬁnement during solid-solid transformations due to thermal cycling29. In the Ti-Cu-Fe25, Ti-Fe, Ti-Cu23, and Ti-6Al-4V-Ni33,34 alloy systems, increasing the solute concentration (Cu, Fe, or Ni) leads to a CET and/ or grain reﬁnement. The increase in solute generally decreases the ΔTs and increases Q and P. Ti-Mo is a more unique example and has been cited as a reason that using Q as a CET indicator is ﬂawed7. Ti-12Mo with a higher Q of 76 K contains columnar grains at the same process parameters as fully equiaxed Ti-4Fe with a lower Q of 42 K (Fig. 4).

produced with similar process conditions are considered (Alloys from Brooke et al.25 and this research (Table S1 and S2)), to obtain greater insight into the various parameters (ΔTs, Q, or P). In Fig. 6c, the parameter P shows the best correlation to a CET, with the linear ﬁt producing an R2 value of 0.81. The produced equation (see Table S5)shows direct proportionality of AR−1 to P where increasing P reduces the area weighted aspect ratio of grains, therefore, increasing P leads to a CET. Compared with ΔTs (Fig. 6a, R2: 0.20) and Q (Fig. 6b, R2: 0.33), P is the best prediction variable. This would indicate that an important consideration is the total amount of CS available in the system (P), rather than the rate of development of this CS (Q). Whereas Q is more

However, Ti-12Mo has a lower P (45 K) compared to Ti-4Fe (111 K). Mendoza et al.35 suggest that the increased absorption of energy for TiMo alloys increases the melt pool size, also slowing cooling rates compared with other Ti alloys. This could help explain the phenomenon of Ti-4Fe producing an equiaxed grain morphology at 600 W, but not Ti-12Mo. It has been shown that manufacturing with vastly different process parameters can change the observed microstructure drastically for DED-LB Ti alloys. Additionally, the grain reﬁnement produced in TiCu binary alloys is also dramatically different from other Ti alloys. Therefore, in Fig. 6a–c, Ti alloys other than Ti-Cu binary alloys

indicative in casting. Ti-12.5Mo produced via casting produces an equiaxed β-grain structure as shown by Ho et al.36, which could be attributed to the large Q. Consideration of the effect on solidiﬁcation rate on the solute partitioning by calculating the modiﬁed partition coefﬁcient (kv)37

for casting, which can be used to predict grain sizes and is a function of 1/Q. These ﬁndings show that 1/P is better correlated to grain size, suggesting updates or modiﬁcations to grain size models are required for AM methods. To further understand the compositional effect on CET, two models were considered for Ti-4Fe, Ti-8Fe, and Ti-12Mo: The Hunt analytical CET model17 (Eqs. 5–6) and the numerically solved model by Kurz et al. and Gäumann et al. (KGT-model)18–21 (Eqs. 5, 7–11). The tip undercooling is seen to vary considerably with increasing solidiﬁcation velocity in the KGT-model. The Hunt model shows an increase in ΔTD with solidiﬁcation velocity, but the Ti-8Fe and Ti12Mo alloys, with similar Q values, have similar values for all velocities. The Ti-4Fe is slightly lower (Fig. 8a), as would be expected. However, considering the KGT-model18–21, the initial tip undercooling appears to be related to the Q value, but this changes as the solidiﬁcation velocity increases. For Ti-12Mo the ΔTD proﬁle follows what would be expected from an alloy with a high Q and low P; at low solidiﬁcation velocities, ΔTD is controlled by Q and is likely to be equiaxed, whilst at higher velocities P is the parameter that more adequately describes the effect of alloy content on the CET, and is likely to be more columnar than the Ti-Fe alloys. Gäumann et al. suggested19 the Hunt CET model17 underestimates the tip undercooling (ΔTD), which is observed here, and suggests the KGT-model is more applicable to AM. At the solidiﬁcation velocities associated with AM, the overall CS (P) is a greater indicator of the CS available to overcome the high thermal gradients of AM. Typically, G-V diagrams indicate the solidiﬁcation conditions that will promote a CET. Shown in Fig. 8b, according to the Hunt model Ti8Fe and Ti-12Mo have a similar propensity to a CET, which is shown not to be the case (Figs. 2 and S3.) Alternatively, the KGT-model shows that at high solidiﬁcation velocities, Ti-12Mo is much more likely to form columnar grains, as seen experimentally. The Hunt analytical model17
was developed to describe the CET of castings. The KGT-model was developed to describe CET during rapid solidiﬁcation, explaining its

(Eq. 4) shows negligible solute trapping effect on the DED-LB Ti alloys as shown in Table 1. A reanalysis of the data of laser-based powder bed fusion (PBF-LB) Al-Si, Al-Cu, and Al-Ni alloys38, also shows P is a better indicator for CET and grain reﬁnement during AM, as shown in Fig. 7a–c. However, at the increased speeds occurring during PBF-LB, the modelling suggests there is some minor effect on the partition coefﬁcient caused by solute trapping. Q and P were recalculated using the modiﬁed partition coefﬁcient, kv shown in Table 1, as Qv and Pv, which are listed in Table 2. Solute trapping may require consideration when designing alloys for PBF-LB; however, again, Pv is best correlated to a CET and grain reﬁnement as shown in Fig. 7d, e. Solute trapping causes less solute rejection, reducing overall CS, therefore, designing for higher P and therefore Pv will promote a CET and grain reﬁnement. As the thermal gradients during PBF-LB are greater than those of DED-LB39, it is likely that the threshold P for a CET would be higher using this manufacturing method. Wire-arc additive manufacturing (WAAM) is generally associated with lower thermal gradients40 therefore less constitutional supercooling is required to overcome the critical undercooling for nucleation to produce a CET. A Cu-30Ni alloy produced via WAAM41 produced a columnar grain morphology as the overall constitutional supercooling (P) is low (see Table 2). Cu-30Ni is a similar case to that of Ti-12Mo (i.e., k > 1), which has a high rate of development of CS (Q) but low overall CS (P), leading to columnar grains during AM. Like Ti-Mo, cast Cu-30Ni can be produced with an equiaxed grain morphology42. Similarly to the CET, grain reﬁnement appears better correlated to 1/P than to the other compositional parameters, as shown in Figs. 3f and 7c. The Interdependence Theory30 is a grain size model developed
Nature Communications| (2025) 16:5710 6

calculations are shown in Table 1. Grain size is used as a proxy for the CET, as more equiaxed grains lead to a smaller grain size as listed and imaged by Tan et al.38. Ordinary least squares linear regression indicates a better correlation to grain size and 1/P and 1/Pv.
Qv and Pv are the Q and P calculated using the modiﬁed partition coefﬁcient kv. Shading corresponds to columnar (dark grey), columnar + equiaxed (light grey), and equiaxed (white) microstructures.

better applicability to AM. Furthermore, the approximation of the G and V values from the Rosenthal equation43 (Eqs. 12–15) (given in Table S6) indicate that the alloy chemistry of Ti-12Mo actually increases the thermal gradient, leading to an increased likelihood of columnar grains. In comparison, the alloy chemistry of Ti-8Fe reduces the thermal gradient, further favouring the CET during AM.

It is evident that the prediction of the CET using CALPHAD data is complex. However, this paper demonstrates that the available CS in an alloy (P) appears to be the more effective parameter when considering the AM of alloys. Solute additions of Cu, Fe, and Ni all lead to a CET in DED-LB Ti alloys, whereas Mo does not. The addition of Cu, Fe, and Ni all effectively increase Q and P, whereas increasing
Nature Communications| (2025) 16:5710 7

Ti-4Fe, Ti-8Fe, and Ti-12Mo produced via DED-LB. Lines represent the transition to the majority columnar microstructure. The coloured point for each alloy (highlighted by the red ellipse) is the Rosenthal approximation of the G and V for the given process parameters and alloy chemistry. The CET lines are tuned to the Rosenthal approximation.

Mo content increases Q much more rapidly than P. ΔTs was not an effective parameter for the prediction of a CET. With increased solidiﬁcation velocity, as is the case in PBF-LB, solute trapping may inﬂuence the amount of solute partitioning. However, in Al-Si, Al-Cu, and Al-Ni alloys, increased P also best predicts a CET. These ﬁndings correlated well with historic models of the CET based on dendrite tip undercoolings. The solute-driven constitutional supercooling is dependent on solidiﬁcation velocity. At high solidiﬁcation velocities (such as during AM) the dendrite tip undercooling correlates more closely with P, in contrast to low solidiﬁcation velocities (as during casting) which correlates more closely with Q. The ﬁndings presented here lay a foundation for alloy design and microstructural control, which are critical for both current and emerging AM technologies.

Methods The ΔTs, Q, and P parameters were calculated from solidiﬁcation simulation using CALPHAD software PANDATTM with the PanTitaniumTM v2022 and PanAluminiumTM v2022 databases. Databases have fully assessed binary systems for Ti-Cu, Ti-Fe, Ti-Mo, Ti-Ni, Al-Si, Al-Ni, Al-Cu and Cu-Ni with the highest reliability rating by PANDATTM. Q was calculated as in Eq. 3 as per ref. 22 (denoted as Qtrue), using Scheil solidiﬁcation. Using the lever rule or Scheil solidiﬁcation will calculate the same Q as fs→022. ΔTs was calculated using PANDATTM

solidiﬁcation simulation as the temperature range from the phase fraction of solid from 0 to 1 using Scheil solidiﬁcation for all compositions. P was calculated using PANDATTM solidiﬁcation simulation, as the temperature range from the phase fraction of solid from 0 to 1, using equilibrium solidiﬁcation for the Ti and Cu alloys. For the Al alloys, as P does not equal the equilibrium freezing range, P was calculated using the calculated Q and Eq. 2. An additional JMatPro Titanium database was used to compare some of the calculations of ΔTs. These were found as the default solidiﬁcation range from the software. The effect of solidiﬁcation rate on the partition coefﬁcient, k, can be approximated by the modiﬁed partition coefﬁcient kv, and is calculated by37:
k + a0v Di
1 + a0v Di

where Di is the solute diffusion coefﬁcient at the solid-liquid interface, a0 is the atomic jump radius, and v is the laser speed. All parameters are listed in Table 1. The Ti alloys use the solute diffusion in liquid (D) as an approximation for Di and the nearest neighbour distance of BCC Ti as an approximation forao. Forthe Al-Si alloys, Di and a0 are taken from work by Qin et al.44, and due to the difﬁculty in measuring diffusion at the solid-liquid interface, the Al-Cu and Al-Ni alloys have assumed similar values. The categorisation of columnar, mixed columnar and equiaxed and equiaxed morphology in this research were made using a calculated area-weighted aspect ratio. Generally, the microstructure is considered equiaxed when the aspect ratio of the grains is less than 2 and columnar when the aspect ratio is greater than 345. As the name suggests, a mixed columnar and equiaxed microstructure features both equiaxed and columnar grains, representing a transitional form between the two morphologies. This grain morphology occurs when the CET is incomplete. The nominal compositions were created by mixing 99.9% pure elemental powders (from ECHKART TLS), which were blended for homogeneity using the TURBULA blender and then manufactured using DED-LB on the Trumpf Trulaser cell 7020. The TruDisk solid state laser (Yb:YAG Disc Laser) with a wavelength of 1030 nm and a Gaussian beam proﬁle was operated under the continuous laser setting. 20 x 10 x 10 mm samples were produced using a laser power of 900 W, a scan speed of 900 mm/min, and a spot size of 1.5 mm. The layer height was 0.25 mm, powder feed rate ~1.8 g/min, track overlap of 0.45 mm, and an interlayer dwell time of 20 s. Ti-4Fe and Ti-12Mo were additionally produced at laser powers of 600 W and 1200 W. Samples were cut along the build direction, resin mounted and polished for microscopy using a JEOL 7200 f SEM. EBSD was collected at 20 kV and at a step size of 2.5 µm for the Ti-Fe and Ti-Cu-Fe samples. Finer step sizes of 1 and 0.5 µm were required for the Ti-6Cu and Ti12.6Cu samples, respectively. Parent grain reconstruction was undertaken using Aztec Crystal software, which implements a version of the method described by Huang et al.46 using the Burgers orientation relationship of the child (α) and parent (β) phases47. A grain boundary threshold angle of 5° was used, and grains were veriﬁed using an overlay of ﬁtted ellipses. Grains were generally easily identiﬁed by the presence of grain boundary α-phase. Only the reconstructed β-grains were measured for the calculation of the area-weighted average grain size. The Ti-12Mo at 900 W and the top layer of Ti-12.6Cu samples were imaged in BSE

ΔTD (Eq. 7) over a limited range of V (1 × 10−5m/s to 1 m/s). The calculated ΔTD for the corresponding V is used in Eq. (5) by assuming ΔTC = ΔTD to solve for the Gc. An approximation of the solidiﬁcation rate V and the thermal gradient G is made using the Rosenthal equation43 for the process parameters and the thermomechanical properties of the alloys. This was similarly undertaken for the additional Ti-6Al-4V+Ni and Ti-18+Fe alloys by Welk et al.33 for their categorisation. with ml being the gradient of the liquidus line (K.wt.%−1), k the partition coefﬁcient, C0 the alloy composition, V the growth rate (m.s−1), Γ the Gibbs-Thomson coefﬁcient (K.m), D the solute diffusion coefﬁcient in the liquid (m2.s−1) and Q is the growth restriction factor (K). The values used for these calculations are provided in Table S7. The true values for ΔTN and N0 are difﬁcult to determine but were rationally chosen to ﬁt the approximated solidiﬁcation velocities and thermal gradient. The Kurz et al. and Gäumann et al.18–21 numerical model (KGTmodel) describing rapid solidiﬁcation considers the dendrite tip temperature as a summation of the solute-driven temperature change and a curvature contribution. The dendrite tip (ΔTD) is calculated in Eq. (7)49 by:

Figure 3 plots the three parameters against the grain size and inverse aspect ratio (AR−1). When comparing these compositional parameters to grain size, the inverse of the parameters was used, as 1/Q has a commonly cited relationship with grain size30,31. From Fig. 3a, d, a clear relationship with ΔTs is not observed, and if anything, increasing ΔTs correlates with grain coarsening.
